ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef tendon food product entry; no specific braised-with-daikon composite dish entry exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bovine meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for braised beef tendon composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised beef tendon with white radish. A0CKP (bovine meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. Daikon component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised beef tendon with white radish as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of braised beef tendon with white radish weighs approximately 250 g and is typically served as a shared dish or individual portion at a dim sum restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, variety meats and by-products, mechanically separated, cooked (SR Legacy) and Radishes, white icicle, raw (SR Legacy); composite estimation for braised beef tendon with daikon dish
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for braised beef tendon with white radish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked beef collagen-rich cuts (beef feet/tendon analogues) and white radish (daikon), combined with typical braising sauce contributions (soy sauce, oyster sauce, rock sugar). Input record values (energy 152 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 8 g, carbohydrate 6 g, sodium 480 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and have been retained. Micronutrient values for calcium (16 mg), iron (1.2 mg), cholesterol (48 mg), vitamin C (4 mg) are also consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar preparations and have been retained.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on braised meat and low-GI vegetable composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised beef tendon with white radish. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on: (1) beef tendon is a protein-dominant ingredient with negligible carbohydrate contribution and effectively zero GI; (2) white radish (daikon) has a published GI of approximately 30 (low-GI vegetable, University of Sydney GI Database); (3) braising sauce contributes small amounts of sugar (rock sugar, oyster sauce) but the overall carbohydrate density remains low at 6 g/100 g. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 6 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 4.5 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 480 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold due to soy sauce and oyster sauce in the braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, avoid consuming the braising liquid, and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation to bring sodium intake within safer limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (6 g/100 g) and low estimated GI (~30) are favourable for glycaemic control, but added sugars from rock sugar and oyster sauce in the braising sauce require monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in moderate portions due to its low GI and high protein content; however, patients should request reduced rock sugar and low-sugar oyster sauce to minimise added sugar intake and should avoid alcohol-containing preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.2 g/100 g and cholesterol at 48 mg/100 g are moderate; sodium at 480 mg/100 g is elevated and poses a cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in small portions (100–150 g), avoid drinking the braising liquid to reduce sodium load, and choose preparations with reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to limit combined saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cinnamon) are beneficial, but high sodium (480 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue swelling and arachidonic acid from red meat may increase omega-6 load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spices in this dish but should limit portion size to reduce arachidonic acid and sodium intake, and request reduced soy sauce during preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Beef Tendon with White Radish (白萝卜炖牛筋) is a classic Chinese comfort dish commonly served at dim sum restaurants in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The dish is prepared by slow-braising beef tendons — a collagen-rich, gelatinous cut — together with chunks of white radish (daikon) in a deeply aromatic spiced broth. The braising liquid typically contains soy sauce, oyster sauce, rock sugar, ginger, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, and sometimes Shaoxing rice wine, producing a rich, savoury-sweet sauce with complex umami depth. After extended braising (typically 2–3 hours), the beef tendon becomes extremely tender and gelatinous, absorbing the flavours of the broth, while the daikon softens and takes on the savoury-sweet braising liquid. The texture of the tendon is uniquely silky and yielding, contrasting with the soft, slightly yielding daikon. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 152 kcal, 14 g protein, 8 g fat (3.2 g saturated), 6 g carbohydrate, 2 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 480 mg sodium. The high collagen content from beef tendon contributes to joint health. The elevated sodium content (480 mg/100 g) is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content and low estimated glycaemic index (~30) make it relatively suitable for glycaemic management in type 2 diabetes when portion size is controlled. Ginger and cinnamon in the braising spices have evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties. This dish is a staple of Cantonese cuisine and is widely available in Singapore hawker centres, dim sum restaurants, and Chinese zi char establishments.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for braised beef tendon with white radish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked collagen-rich beef cuts and white radish, combined with typical braising sauce contributions. The input record values are consistent with this estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI profile of daikon and the negligible carbohydrate contribution of beef tendon. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for braised beef tendon with white radish composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific braised composite dish is not reported in available approved sources; estimated ~200 mg/100 g from daikon and beef components but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not available for this specific braised composite dish in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.2 g/100 g from the input record, consistent with small amounts of naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant beef products. All core macros (protein_g 14.0, fat_g 8.0, carbohydrate_g 6.0) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification. Halal status is marked false as this dish commonly contains oyster sauce (shellfish) and may contain Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol); halal-certified versions may be available on request.
